title: Moving Cooling Source Induced Phase Separation in Binary Liquids: an interplay of competing velocities
authors: Lakshmipriya K, Harssh Karn, Sutapa Roy
abstract: We investigate phase separation dynamics in a binary mixture subjected to a moving cooling source from which cold temperature fronts propagate radially outward into the mixture. The motion of the source introduces two distinct velocity scales: $v_s$ associated with the translation of the source, and $v$ related to the propagation of the cooling thermal fronts. Competition between the two velocities determines how long a region of the fluid experiences a temperature change, which directly controls phase separation. A modified Cahn Hilliard Cook framework is employed, incorporating explicit coupling between the time-dependent temperature and concentration fields. Our numerical simulation results reveal that the evolving patterns and kinetics strongly depend on both the ratio and absolute magnitudes of these two competing velocities. Same value of $v_s/v$ yields distinctly different patterns for different $v$. The temperature profile delineating spatial regions with local temperatures above and below the demixing temperature controls the shape of the patterns formed. The rich parameter space enables one to engineer desired pattern structures by tuning the two velocities.
summary: This paper explores how the motion of a cooling source influences the patterns formed during the phase separation of a binary liquid. By comparing the speed of the moving source to the speed of the spreading cold front, the authors demonstrate that different geometric structures, such as rings or leaf-like domains, can be produced. The findings suggest that one can precisely engineer specific patterns by controlling these two velocity scales. This work provides a framework for understanding non-equilibrium phase separation in driven, inhomogeneous systems.
main_problem: Investigating how the competition between the translation velocity of a moving cooling source and the radial propagation velocity of thermal fronts determines the pattern morphology and kinetics of phase separation in binary liquids.
main_result: The study identifies distinct morphological regimes (concentric rings, asymmetric stripes, and leaf-like structures) controlled by the velocity ratio and absolute magnitudes of the source and front velocities, showing that pattern engineering is possible by tuning these scales.
method: Numerical simulations using a finite-difference method to solve a modified Cahn-Hilliard-Cook framework.
model_or_system: A 2D binary mixture undergoing phase separation, modeled via a Cahn-Hilliard-Cook equation that explicitly couples a time-dependent temperature field (generated by a moving source) with a concentration field.
key_observables: Order parameter (concentration difference), domain morphology (shape and symmetry), number of stripes (N), domain width (w), and the two-point equal-time correlation function.
important_parameters: Velocity of the cooling source (vs), velocity of the thermal front (v), velocity ratio (gamma = vs/v), and initial composition (psi_0).
main_assumptions: The temperature field is driven by a moving source with radial propagation; the study focuses on the non-equilibrium dynamics of the demixing transition.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the evolution of concentric rings and the transition to semi-circular stripes; Figure 2 illustrates vertical stripe-like structures and the linear growth of the number of stripes; Figure 3 displays asymmetric leaf-like morphologies; Figure 4 and 5 demonstrate the linear temporal growth of temperature front width and domain width.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of moving cooling sources, describes the Cahn-Hilliard-Cook model and numerical setup, presents results categorized by velocity regimes (Case I, II, and III), analyzes scaling and kinetics, and concludes with implications for pattern engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A moving cooling source induced phase separation in a binary mixture with an off-critical composition, ψ0 = 0.1. At t = 0, the source is positioned at (L/3, L/2) and translates along positive x direction with a constant speed vs. The cold temperature front propagates radially outward from the source with a constant speed v. L = 1000, dt = 0.001, Ti = 1, and Ts = −1. (a): Coarsening patterns for v = 0.3 and vs = 0.01 are shown at four representative times. The color code represents the order parameter ψ, where ψ > 0 and < 0 correspond to the A and B rich phase, respectively. Near the source, concentric circular rings of alternate phases emerge. As time progresses, additional rings...

FIG. 2. (a) Coarsening patterns for v = 0.3, vs = 0.3 at four representative times are shown for L = 1000, Ti = 1, Ts = −1. The position of the moving cooling source is indicated by a solid line. Circular domains emerge, consisting of stripes of alternating phases. As time progresses, the domain size increases along with the number of stripes. (b) Temperature contours corresponding to the same parameters as in (a) are shown at two representative times. These contours delineate regions where a(r, t) > 0 and < 0. (c) Double logarithmic plot of the number of stripes N vs. time t for v = 0.3, vs = 0.3, Ti = 1, Ts = −1 for four different system sizes. Symbols refer to numerical data and the...

FIG. 3. (a) Phase separation patterns for v = 0.3 and vs = 0.4 at four different times are shown. In the vicinity of the cooling source, an asymmetric, leaf-like domain emerges, consisting of stripes of alter- nating phases. With increasing time, additional stripes form and the overall domain size increases. For clarity, only a portion of the simu- lation domain is displayed. (b) Temperature contours corresponding to the same parameters as in (a) are shown at three representative times. The leaf-shaped contour, arising from the condition vs > v, closely correlates with the domain morphology. As time progresses, the source moves rightward and the contour becomes larger.

FIG. 4. (a) Plot of the spatial region wT over which the cold tem- perature front propagates, defined by the condition a(r, t) < 0, vs. time t, on a double-logarithmic scale. Solid line stands for a linear growth. Inset: same plot for a stationary source (vs = 0).
FIG. 5. Log–log plot of the domain width w as a function of time for fixed v and three different vs.

FIG. 7. Domain morphology for (a) v = 0.1, vs = 0.01 and (b) v = 0.1, vs = 0.1, for L = 1000, Ti = 1, Ts = −1. Translational speed of the cooling source vs influences the direction of bending of the stripes. (c) Domain morphology for v = 0.1 and vs = 0.2 is shown at a representative time. The significantly larger source velocity vs (as compared to the front velocity v) yields strongly asymmetric pattern.

FIG. 6. Scaled two-point equal time correlation function C(r, t)/C(r = 0, t), is shown at three representative times for v = 0.3 and two different values of vs. Data from different times are marked by different symbols.

FIG. 8. Phase diagram of the system in the v−vs parameter plane for ψ0 = 0.1. All data refer to Ti = 1, Ts = −1. Symbols correspond to distinct pattern shapes- square: concentric circular rings, diamond: semicircular stripes pattern, triangle up: circular domain with verti- cal stripes, circle: asymmetric domain with stripes.
FIG. 10. Pattern morphologies at critical composition ψ0 = 0, for different combinations of v and vs.

FIG. 9. Domain morphology for v = 0.3, vs = 0.4 for a cooling source moving along the y direction. Initially the source is placed at (L/2, L/3). The solid line represents the propagation of the cooling source.
